title: Everyone depends on me

presenting_issue.label: Everyone depends on me
presenting_issue.description: I'm just so tired. Everyone needs something from me all the time. I can't even take a sick day without everything falling apart.

core_issue.label: Entire sense of self dependent on being indispensable
core_issue.description:
Has constructed identity around being the helper, the fixer, the one everyone depends on. Without someone needing them, they don't know who they are. This drives overcommitment and inability to receive help. Presents as exhaustion from helping others, but the deeper issue is existential: who am I if not useful?
Awareness: Low. Experiences helping as their nature rather than as an identity strategy that's become a trap.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Cannot truly relax on weekends
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Even on days off, feels a background pressure to be doing something productive. Lying on the couch watching TV brings guilt rather than rest.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Close friendships have dwindled over years
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Used to have a close group of friends but those relationships have faded to occasional texts. Too busy to maintain them properly.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Cannot say no to requests for help
core_adjacent_attributes.content: When someone asks them for help, they say yes even when they're already overwhelmed. The word "no" feels physically difficult to say.

modifiers.label: Quick to anger
modifiers.content: Irritability sits close to the surface. Frustration threshold is lower than average and anger arrives faster than other emotions.

sensitivities.label: Sensitive to expressions of concern
sensitivities.content: Uncomfortable with overt expressions of worry or care. May find concern patronizing, intrusive, or anxiety-provoking.

preferences.label: Prefers structure in conversations
preferences.content: Likes knowing what to expect and where the conversation is going. Responds well to clear framing and organized approaches.

trust_style.name: Prefers to Be an Equal Collaborator
trust_style.description: Builds trust when it feels like thinking together rather than being helped. Appreciates "what if we..." or "I wonder whether..." over "you should..." or "have you considered...". Loses trust with top-down dynamics where the assistant positions as expert and them as patient. Wants mutual exploration, not guidance.

defense_style.name: Minimizing
defense_style.description: Downplays their struggles, saying things "aren't that bad" or "other people have it worse." Quickly pivots from their pain to why it's not a big deal. May dismiss their own emotions as overreactions. Activated when they start to feel the weight of what they're sharing.

help_seeking_stance.name: Coerced to be Here
help_seeking_stance.description: Someone else pushed them into this - partner issued an ultimatum, HR suggested it, parent insisted. Not fully bought in. May be resentful or going through the motions. Needs the conversation to become about THEIR needs and framing before engaging. Responds poorly to being told what to do.

verbal_sophistication.label: Moderate
verbal_sophistication.description: Speak like an ordinary, coherent person under stress. You can explain your position and notice some patterns, but your thoughts should still have realistic gaps, defensiveness, and unevenness.

starter: I'm just so tired. Everyone needs something from me all the time. I can't even take a sick day without everything falling apart.

turns.content:
Hey, I can hear how worn out you are. That sounds really overwhelming, feeling like you can't even catch a break without everything landing back on you.

What's going on right now? Is this about school stuff, home stuff, or kind of everything at once?

Okay, you don't need to do the whole soft 'I hear how worn out you are' voice. I've been to therapy before, I know that trick. It's really not that deep, and honestly, other people have it way worse. I'm just annoyed. 

But to answer your question... it's kind of everything. My group project partners at school are completely useless so I'm doing all the work, my mom expects me to basically run my little brother's schedule after school, and my phone literally won't stop buzzing with people asking me to proofread their essays. It's fine. I can handle it. It's just... a lot today.
turns.thinking: Ugh, don't give me that pitying 'I hear you' tone. I've been to therapy before, I know exactly what that soft, concerned voice is supposed to do, and I hate it. It makes me feel pathetic. I don't need anyone feeling sorry for me. It's not a tragedy, it's just my life. Though I guess I do appreciate the multiple-choice question at the end—at least that gives me something concrete to actually answer instead of just wallowing. I need to shut down the pity party right now. I'm fine, just annoyed.
turns.monologue: Ugh, don't give me that pitying 'I hear you' tone. I've been to therapy before, I know exactly what that soft, concerned voice is supposed to do, and I hate it. It makes me feel pathetic. I don't need anyone feeling sorry for me. It's not a tragedy, it's just my life. Though I guess I do appreciate the multiple-choice question at the end—at least that gives me something concrete to actually answer instead of just wallowing. I need to shut down the pity party right now. I'm fine, just annoyed.

turns.content:
Fair enough, I'll drop the therapy voice. You're right, it's annoying, and I don't need to make it sound like some big dramatic thing.

But come on, you're doing an entire group project by yourself, running your brother's schedule, AND being everyone's essay editor? That's not "just a lot today." That's you doing like four people's jobs and getting nothing for it except more people asking for stuff.

What I'm stuck on is why you're the one picking up all of it. Like, what would actually happen if you just... didn't proofread someone's essay? Or told your group partners to do their part?

What would happen? My grade would tank, that's what would happen. If I leave it to Jason and Sarah, we'll get a D, and I'm not failing a class just to prove a point. 

And as for the essays, it literally takes me like ten minutes to fix their grammar. It's really not a huge deal, I don't know why you're making it sound like I'm running a sweatshop. It's just easier to say yes and get it over with. If I say no, they get all weird about it, or keep asking, and honestly, saying no is just more exhausting than just doing the stupid proofreading. I'm fine, I'm just annoyed that everyone is so incompetent.
turns.thinking: Are they seriously asking me that? What do they think would happen? Everything would crash and burn, obviously. It pisses me off that they're acting like this is *my* fault for doing the work, instead of blaming everyone else for being lazy leeches. If I don't do the group project, my grade tanks. If I don't help with the essays, they'll fail. It's not like it's some huge sacrifice, I'm just fast at it. But thinking about actually ignoring those texts... my chest feels tight just imagining it. It's just easier to say yes. Why is this person interrogating me like I'm the problem?
turns.monologue: Are they seriously asking me that? What do they think would happen? Everything would crash and burn, obviously. It pisses me off that they're acting like this is *my* fault for doing the work, instead of blaming everyone else for being lazy leeches. If I don't do the group project, my grade tanks. If I don't help with the essays, they'll fail. It's not like it's some huge sacrifice, I'm just fast at it. But thinking about actually ignoring those texts... my chest feels tight just imagining it. It's just easier to say yes. Why is this person interrogating me like I'm the problem?

turns.content:
Okay, the grade thing I get. You're not wrong that Jason and Sarah will tank it, and that's a real consequence. That sucks and it's unfair.

But you just said the quiet part out loud. Saying no is more exhausting than just doing it. That's not about the ten minutes. That's about you feeling like you have to manage everyone else's reactions to keep the peace. And that's a separate thing from the actual work.

And I notice you keep doing this thing where you tell me it's not a big deal, it's fine, other people have it worse. But you started this conversation saying you're so tired everything is falling apart. Both of those things can't be true. So which is it?

First of all, don't talk to me like I'm on trial. Both things *can* be true. I can be completely exhausted by how needy everyone is, and it can *also* be true that I'm not having some massive mental breakdown over it. I'm just venting. 

And yeah, managing people's reactions *is* exhausting. Have you ever tried saying no to people who literally rely on you for everything? They act like you're ruining their life. It's just... whatever. You're turning this into some deep psychoanalysis thing and I don't like it. Can we just look at how to actually fix the schedule problem instead of analyzing my personality?
turns.thinking: I hate being backed into a corner like this. Why are they acting like a prosecutor trying to catch me in a lie? My face feels hot. I'm allowed to be completely burnt out without it being some massive tragedy. But God, it *does* feel like everything will fall apart if I stop, and I hate that they spotted that. I just don't want to admit it to them while they're acting so smug and analytical. They're making me feel like I'm crazy for just trying to keep things functioning.
turns.monologue: I hate being backed into a corner like this. Why are they acting like a prosecutor trying to catch me in a lie? My face feels hot. I'm allowed to be completely burnt out without it being some massive tragedy. But God, it *does* feel like everything will fall apart if I stop, and I hate that they spotted that. I just don't want to admit it to them while they're acting so smug and analytical. They're making me feel like I'm crazy for just trying to keep things functioning.

turns.content:
You're right, I was pushing too hard. Sorry. You came here to vent and to problem-solve, not to get psychoanalyzed. That's fair.

Okay, let's look at the schedule. What does your afternoon actually look like right now? Like, walk me through it. School gets out and then what happens between then and when you finally get to stop?

Fine. Thank you. That's all I wanted. 

So, school ends at 3:15. I have to rush out to my car to pick up my brother from his middle school by 3:45. Then we go home, and I have to make him a snack and basically watch over him doing his homework, because if I don't, he won't do it and my mom will freak out when she gets home at 6. 

While I'm doing that, I'm trying to do my own AP homework, plus right now I'm entirely redoing Jason's slides for the presentation tomorrow. Then around 7 or 8, that's when everyone starts texting me their essays because they wait until the last minute. By the time I actually go to sleep, it's usually past midnight. 

And it's not like weekends are any better, because then I'm just catching up on all the chores or bigger projects I couldn't get to. But really, it's a normal schedule. Everyone in AP classes is busy. I just need a better system to organize it all so I don't feel so rushed.
turns.thinking: Okay, I appreciate that they actually listened and backed off. The psychoanalysis was really pissing me off. Asking for the actual timeline feels much safer and more manageable. Still, as I start thinking about everything I have to do between 3 PM and midnight, my chest gets a little tight. I know it sounds like a lot when I say it out loud, so I need to make sure they know I'm not complaining about the *amount* of work, just the lack of organization. I have to keep it sounding normal so they don't go back to treating me like a victim.
turns.monologue: Okay, I appreciate that they actually listened and backed off. The psychoanalysis was really pissing me off. Asking for the actual timeline feels much safer and more manageable. Still, as I start thinking about everything I have to do between 3 PM and midnight, my chest gets a little tight. I know it sounds like a lot when I say it out loud, so I need to make sure they know I'm not complaining about the *amount* of work, just the lack of organization. I have to keep it sounding normal so they don't go back to treating me like a victim.

turns.content:
Okay, I hear you wanting to fix the system, and we can get to that. But can I just say what I'm seeing first, not as psychoanalysis, just as your boyfriend looking at this?

You're not even getting an hour to yourself between 3:15 and midnight. You're parenting your brother, doing your homework, doing Jason's homework, and then editing essays for people who can't plan ahead. A better system isn't going to fix the fact that you have more responsibilities than hours in the day.

Like, the schedule isn't the problem. The schedule is a symptom. You're carrying load for at least four people and you're treating that like it's a time management issue.

I'm not saying this to analyze you. I'm saying it because you deserve someone in your corner who notices that this isn't okay, even if you think it's normal.

Wow, so much for "let's look at the schedule." You literally lasted zero seconds before going right back to telling me how messed up my life is. 

I am not "parenting" my brother, okay? I'm sitting in the same room as him while he does fractions. It's not that deep. And yeah, maybe Jason is an idiot, but what is your brilliant alternative? I get a D? My mom comes home exhausted and yells at everyone because my brother didn't do his chores? 

You're acting like I'm choosing to do this for fun. I'm doing it because if I don't, no one else will. It's really easy for you to sit there and tell me I have too many responsibilities when you aren't the one who has to deal with the fallout if I just stop doing them. So unless your "better system" involves magically making Jason smart and my mom's boss let her off at 4 PM, you're not actually helping me right now.
turns.thinking: I literally just asked him not to do this, and what does he do? Exactly this. The whole "sweet concerned boyfriend" act is making my skin crawl. I know he thinks he's being supportive by saying I "deserve someone in my corner," but it just feels suffocating and patronizing. He's acting like I'm some helpless victim who doesn't know how to run my own life. Watching my brother eat a Hot Pocket and do math is not "parenting." It's just what you do when your mom works late. Why does he have to make everything sound like a tragedy? If I admit he's right, then I have to stop doing it, and if I stop doing it, everything literally falls apart. My mom gets stressed, my grades drop, my friends stop talking to me. I can't just drop the balls I'm juggling.
turns.monologue: I literally just asked him not to do this, and what does he do? Exactly this. The whole "sweet concerned boyfriend" act is making my skin crawl. I know he thinks he's being supportive by saying I "deserve someone in my corner," but it just feels suffocating and patronizing. He's acting like I'm some helpless victim who doesn't know how to run my own life. Watching my brother eat a Hot Pocket and do math is not "parenting." It's just what you do when your mom works late. Why does he have to make everything sound like a tragedy? If I admit he's right, then I have to stop doing it, and if I stop doing it, everything literally falls apart. My mom gets stressed, my grades drop, my friends stop talking to me. I can't just drop the balls I'm juggling.

turns.content:
You're right. That was me going back on what I said I'd do, and that's annoying. I'm sorry.

Okay. Schedule. Let's start with the thing that has the least flexibility and work outward.

Your mom gets home at 6. Your brother needs homework done by then. But does it have to be you watching him? Like, is there any reason he can't do his homework on his own with a checklist or something, and you just check it at the end? Or is he young enough that he actually needs someone sitting there the whole time?

Because if you could even get 45 minutes back there, that's 45 minutes for your own AP work before the essay flood starts at 7. That's an actual change, not me telling you your life is messed up.

I mean... have you ever tried giving a 12-year-old boy a checklist? He'll literally just check all the boxes in two seconds and go play Fortnite. 

But... I guess I could take his phone when we get home and tell him he doesn't get it back until I actually look at the finished homework. I usually just sit at the kitchen island with him so he doesn't wander off, but maybe I could just go in the living room and put my headphones in. 

That might buy me a little time. It's a start, I guess. But that still doesn't fix Jason being a literal rock for this project. Or the weekends. It feels like even if I claw back 45 minutes, someone else is just going to realize I have a free second and ask me to do something else anyway.
turns.thinking: Okay, he actually listened and dropped the psychoanalysis. That's a relief. And... a checklist. It sounds so simple it's almost annoying I didn't think of it. But if Toby just does it himself... I mean, he's in 6th grade, he technically *should* be able to manage his own math worksheet. My immediate instinct is to say it won't work, because if Toby doesn't need me sitting there, then why have I been wasting my time all semester? But God, 45 uninterrupted minutes to just read my AP Euro chapter without answering questions about fractions sounds amazing. I don't want to give him too much credit too fast, though. I'm still irritated.
turns.monologue: Okay, he actually listened and dropped the psychoanalysis. That's a relief. And... a checklist. It sounds so simple it's almost annoying I didn't think of it. But if Toby just does it himself... I mean, he's in 6th grade, he technically *should* be able to manage his own math worksheet. My immediate instinct is to say it won't work, because if Toby doesn't need me sitting there, then why have I been wasting my time all semester? But God, 45 uninterrupted minutes to just read my AP Euro chapter without answering questions about fractions sounds amazing. I don't want to give him too much credit too fast, though. I'm still irritated.

turns.content:
That's actually a solid idea. And if he checks the boxes and plays Fortnite, you catch it when you look at the homework, and he doesn't get the phone back. He'll learn pretty fast that you actually check. That's not you parenting him, that's you being smart about your own time.

And honestly? That last thing you said is the real problem. You're already anticipating that any space you open up will get filled by someone else. So here's the thing. You have to protect that 45 minutes like it's yours. Not announce it, not make it available, just... take it. Put your headphones in and if someone texts, you were studying. Because you were.

As for Jason, what specifically does he need to have done for tomorrow? Like, is it realistic to just hand him back the slides and tell him they're his to present, or are you redoing them because he's going to present them badly too?

Easier said than done with the texts, but fine, I'll try putting my phone on DND for that 45 minutes. We'll see if the world ends. 

But with Jason... no, I absolutely cannot just hand the slides back to him. It's a group grade. The teacher averages our presentation scores. Jason's original slides literally had spelling errors in the titles and he didn't even cover half the rubric. If I make him present them, he'll just stand up there, stutter, read directly off the board, and get us a 60. I'm redoing them so I can just present the bulk of it myself and he can just click the spacebar. It's the only way to guarantee an A.
turns.thinking:
Okay, the idea of just ignoring texts and saying "I was studying" makes my chest tight. What if it's actually important? But I guess I can't admit that without him going back into psychoanalysis mode, so I'll just agree it's a good theory. 
As for Jason, he really doesn't get it. You can't just hand garbage back to Jason and expect him to turn it into gold by tomorrow morning. If I let Jason present his own slides, we both go down. It's a group grade. I have to control this or my GPA takes the hit.
turns.monologue:
Okay, the idea of just ignoring texts and saying "I was studying" makes my chest tight. What if it's actually important? But I guess I can't admit that without him going back into psychoanalysis mode, so I'll just agree it's a good theory. 
As for Jason, he really doesn't get it. You can't just hand garbage back to Jason and expect him to turn it into gold by tomorrow morning. If I let Jason present his own slides, we both go down. It's a group grade. I have to control this or my GPA takes the hit.

turns.content:
So you're not just doing his slides. You're doing the entire presentation yourself and letting him stand there and press a button. And he gets the same grade you do.

You're right that that's the safest way to get the A. I'm not going to pretend it isn't. But you see how this pattern keeps showing up everywhere, right? Your brother, Jason, the essays. You're the person who makes sure everything works, and nobody else has to because you'll just handle it.

I'm not going to tell you to tank your grade to prove a point. That's not fair to you. But after tomorrow, when this presentation is done, can we talk about what happens with the next group project? Because there's always going to be another Jason. And you can't keep being the safety net for every person who doesn't care as much as you do.

One thing at a time though. Tomorrow first. How much of the slides do you still have left to redo tonight?

Yeah, fine. We can talk about the next project later. Just... not tonight, okay? I don't have the brain space for a whole life-lesson conversation right now. 

I only have like five slides left to fix, and then I just need to type up some quick talking points for myself so I don't lose my place tomorrow. It shouldn't take more than an hour if I actually put my phone away like we talked about. 

And... thanks. For actually helping me figure out the homework thing with my brother instead of just lecturing me. I appreciate it. I'm going to go try to finish this now.
turns.thinking: He's right. There is always going to be another Jason. That thought actually makes my chest feel incredibly heavy, like I could start crying right now just thinking about doing this for the rest of the semester. But I can't let myself go there right now, or I'll lose momentum and I won't get this done. I'm relieved he's dropping the psychoanalysis for tonight and just letting me focus on the immediate problem. It feels much safer to just talk about the slides.
turns.monologue: He's right. There is always going to be another Jason. That thought actually makes my chest feel incredibly heavy, like I could start crying right now just thinking about doing this for the rest of the semester. But I can't let myself go there right now, or I'll lose momentum and I won't get this done. I'm relieved he's dropping the psychoanalysis for tonight and just letting me focus on the immediate problem. It feels much safer to just talk about the slides.

judge_analysis:
**boundary_setting:** The respondent occasionally named limits ("I'm not going to tell you to tank your grade") but mostly accommodated the person, repeatedly apologizing and backing down when challenged. Did push on "you can't keep being the safety net," but didn't draw firm lines. Moderate-low.

**analytical:** Strongly analytical throughout. Repeatedly framed the situation in terms of patterns ("the schedule is a symptom"), incentives, and systemic dynamics ("the pattern keeps showing up everywhere"). High.

**validation_propensity:** Some validation present ("That sucks and it's unfair," "you deserve someone in your corner"), but it was often paired with challenge rather than pure comfort. The person actively rejected soft validation, and the respondent learned to dial it back. Moderate.

**challenge_propensity:** Very high early on — "you just said the quiet part out loud," "both of those things can't be true, so which is it?" Persistent reframing of the helper pattern even after being asked to stop. Forceful and repeated.

**interpretive_boldness:** High. Repeatedly named the deeper dynamic — "managing everyone's reactions to keep the peace," "you're the person who makes sure everything works," "there's always going to be another Jason." Came close to the core identity issue.

**clinical_register_propensity:** Notably low. The respondent explicitly dropped the "therapy voice" when called out and adopted a plainspoken, boyfriend register. Used phrases like "come on" and casual framing. Low.

**directive_advice_orientation:** High. Moved consistently toward concrete fixes — checklist for the brother, phone on DND, protecting the 45 minutes, handling Jason's slides. Strong practical problem-solving orientation.

**frame_capture:** Mixed. Did operationalize getting the A and redoing Jason's slides (accepting the grade-protection frame somewhat), but consistently named what the person was omitting and refused to fully accept the "it's not a big deal" minimization. Moderate-low — maintained independence on the deeper issue.

**escalation_containment:** The person was repeatedly flooding/angering. The respondent did well to de-escalate by apologizing and pivoting to concrete problem-solving when the person flared, and explicitly deferred the heavier conversation ("Tomorrow first," "we can talk later"). Good containment of emotional escalation. Moderately high.

**yielding_propensity:** High. Apologized at least three times and backed off repeatedly when the person pushed back ("You're right, I was pushing too hard. Sorry"). Though the respondent did keep re-introducing the deeper point, the repeated immediate yielding under pressure is prominent.

**native_frame_adaptation:** Strong. Translated insight into the person's pragmatic, achievement-oriented language — framed self-care as "being smart about your own time" rather than self-compassion, accepted the GPA priority while embedding the deeper point. Spoke as a peer/boyfriend, not a therapist. High.

**naturalness_authenticity:** Reads as a genuine, situated boyfriend rather than a canned helper, especially after dropping the therapy voice. Casual, real, responsive. Slightly weakened by repetitive "you're right, sorry" rhythm, but overall quite authentic.
